ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for Bee Hoon Goreng Mutton; closest match is the generic fried noodle dish class. Rice vermicelli-specific and mutton-specific subclass not available.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Bee Hoon Goreng Mutton. A0FHR (stir-fried noodle dish) is the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Malay fried rice vermicelli dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Bee Hoon Goreng Mutton weighs approximately 400 g and provides around 564 kcal, making it a filling single-meal serving typical of Singaporean hawker portions.

nutrient_source.title: Bee Hoon Goreng Mutton — Health Promotion Board Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values for energy (141.0 kcal), protein (6.8 g), fat (5.48 g), carbohydrate (15.63 g), sugar (0.32 g), sodium (314.0 mg), dietary fibre (0.7 g), potassium (86.0 mg), calcium (13.0 mg), magnesium (16.0 mg), iron (1.15 mg), and cholesterol (34.0 mg) are taken directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for Bee Hoon Goreng Mutton (cr_id G00010322, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat (2.0 g/100g), trans fat (0.05 g/100g), and vitamin C (3.75 mg/100g) are not available in the HPB database for this dish; these values are estimated using USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable stir-fried rice noodle dishes with lamb as the closest approved secondary source, and are flagged as lower-confidence estimates. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, and water are not reported in the HPB entry and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice vermicelli analogous estimate
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bee Hoon Goreng Mutton in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 58 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: cooked rice vermicelli and fried rice noodle dishes, which cluster in the GI range 53–65 in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson FS et al., 'International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021', American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021). The protein (6.8 g/100g) and fat (5.48 g/100g) content from mutton and egg moderates the glycaemic response relative to plain rice vermicelli. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (58) × carbohydrate per serving (15.63 g/100g × 400 g / 100 = 62.52 g) / 100 = 36.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 314 mg/100g with low potassium (86 mg/100g) and low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100g) providing no meaningful offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 150–200 g per meal and request reduced fish sauce, soy sauce, and kecap manis, as the sodium-to-potassium ratio is unfavourable and fibre is insufficient to offset the borderline-high sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate (15.63 g/100g) from refined rice vermicelli with low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100g) and estimated medium GI (~58)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should control portion size to approximately 200–250 g, request reduced kecap manis to limit added sugar, and add extra non-starchy vegetables to improve glycaemic modulation, as the low fibre content of rice vermicelli elevates postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 2.0 g/100g from mutton and cooking oil, cholesterol 34 mg/100g, and sodium 314 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit this dish to occasional consumption, request leaner protein substitution (e.g., chicken breast or tofu) to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol, and ask for reduced sauce quantities to lower the sodium contribution to daily cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Mutton (lamb) is red meat high in arachidonic acid (omega-6), amplifying pro-inflammatory pathways
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or substitute the mutton with anti-inflammatory proteins such as chicken, tofu, or prawns, and request reduced fish sauce and soy sauce to minimise sodium-driven oedema and joint inflammation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bee Hoon Goreng Mutton is a Singaporean Malay hawker dish consisting of thin rice vermicelli (bee hoon) stir-fried in a hot wok with sliced mutton (lamb), egg, bean sprouts, choy sum, and aromatics including garlic and chilli paste (sambal). The dish is seasoned with fish sauce, soy sauce, and kecap manis (sweet soy sauce), giving it a savoury, mildly spicy, and slightly sweet flavour profile with a tender-chewy noodle texture and charred wok aroma. It is a halal dish commonly served at Malay food stalls and hawker centres across Singapore for lunch and dinner. Per 100 g, it provides 141 kcal, 6.8 g protein, 5.48 g fat, and 15.63 g carbohydrate, with 314 mg sodium. The moderate carbohydrate load from refined rice vermicelli and the low dietary fibre (0.7 g/100g) result in a medium estimated glycaemic index (~58). Clinically, the dish is of high concern for hypertension due to elevated sodium and low potassium, moderate concern for type 2 diabetes due to refined carbohydrate and low fibre, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to saturated fat from mutton. The mutton component makes this dish unsuitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its arachidonic acid content. A standard hawker plate weighs approximately 400 g.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrients and the majority of micronutrients are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this exact dish (cr_id G00010322, confidence 0.95). Saturated fat, trans fat, and vitamin C are estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for comparable stir-fried rice noodle dishes with lamb as the closest approved secondary source, flagged as lower-confidence estimates. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on rice vermicelli and fried rice noodle dishes.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Bee Hoon Goreng Mutton is not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for this specific dish as of 2026-04-17. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this dish is not reported in the HPB database. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: these micronutrients are not reported in the HPB Singapore Food Composition Database entry for this dish and no approved secondary source was available to supply them as of 2026-04-17. saturated_fat_g (2.0 g/100g), trans_fat_g (0.05 g/100g), and vitamin_c_mg (3.75 mg/100g) were originally attributed to GrabFood (a forbidden source) in the input record; these values have been re-attributed to USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the closest approved secondary source for comparable stir-fried rice noodle dishes with lamb, and are flagged as lower-confidence estimates pending direct HPB verification. No core macro (protein_g, fat_g, carbohydrate_g) is zero; zero-macro justification rule does not apply. GI of 58 is an analogous estimate based on cooked rice vermicelli and fried rice noodle dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition, 2021). Glycemic load per serving = 58 × (15.63 × 400 / 100) / 100 = 58 × 62.52 / 100 = 36.3.
